_comment: Reference catalog for every user-facing string the renderers emit (HTML report + widget card). To add a language, copy this file to <code>.json (e.g. de.json) in this directory, translate the values, and set "locale" in config.json (or pass --locale). Missing keys fall back to English. {name} placeholders are str.format parameters and must be kept. Keys whose values contain HTML tags/entities are inserted as raw HTML by the report (methodology + recommendations copy); everything else is plain text and gets escaped by the renderer.
axis.codl.name: CODL
axis.codl.description: How many agent sessions you're supervising at once. Working memory caps at about 4 concurrent threads (Cowan 2001).
axis.codl.technique: Sweep over per-stream (first_ts, last_ts) intervals sampled at 1-min resolution within the configured work window.
axis.codl.basis: Cowan (2001); Cummings & Mitchell (2008) on supervisory-control fan-out.
axis.codl.caveat: Streams treated as continuously alive between first/last event; long mid-session gaps are over-counted as engaged time.
axis.interruption.name: Interruption Index
axis.interruption.description: Weighted attention-pulling events per work hour: tool errors (need user intervention) and cross-session switches. Mark (2008) showed interrupted work is faster but more stressful.
axis.interruption.technique: tool_error × 1.5 + cross-stream-start × 3.0, divided by work-hour duration. Tool calls inside a session are intentionally NOT counted — when the agent is using a tool the supervisor is in a Waiting state, not being interrupted.
axis.interruption.basis: Mark, Gudith & Klocke (2008); Mark, Gonzalez & Harris (2005); Leroy (2009) on attention residue.
axis.interruption.caveat: Tool-use timestamps not preserved at the aggregate layer; events are apportioned by stream-active-time overlap with the work window.
axis.closure.name: Closure Deficit
axis.closure.description: Loops you couldn't finish in one sitting and had to pick back up. Each resume costs more the longer the session sat parked. 0 = every loop closed in one sitting, 1 = many cold reloads. Lower is better.
axis.closure.technique: Sum of per-resume severities ÷ a daily ceiling, clipped to [0,1]. A resume is a true-idle gap in a session (no activity at all) of ≥ 30 min, or a pickup on a later day; each resume's severity is min(1, gap ÷ 120 min), so cost rises with how long the loop was parked then saturates once context is fully cold. Scored on every active day; independent of the CODL shape.
axis.closure.basis: Monk, Trafton & Boehm-Davis (2008) resumption cost rises with gap duration; Altmann & Trafton (2002) goal-activation decay; Parnin & Rugaber (2011) in-domain reconstruction tax; Sonnentag & Fritz (2007) closure as recovery.
axis.closure.caveat: A gap is dormant wall-clock time, a proxy for a parked loop — a long autonomous agent turn is excluded (it's not idle), but stepping away with a session still mid-turn isn't. The lab evidence measured short (sub-minute) gaps; multi-hour and cross-day gaps extrapolate beyond that regime, with Parnin & Rugaber as the closest field bridge.
axis.unit.closure_not_scored: not scored — no activity this day
workwindow.label: work window: {start} – {end}
nag.off_hours: ↑ +{points} pts from {minutes} min of off-hours work
card.chart_title: Max concurrent agent sessions per hour
card.axes_frozen: metrics only update during working hours
report.title: Cognitive Stress Profile — {label}
report.heading: Cognitive Stress Profile
report.source_stats: · {active} active days / {window} in window · {events} events from {records} records
report.year_note: Monthly cells show the average composite score over the month's active workdays. The line below shows each workday as a bar colored by its composite score (same palette as the calendar heatmap). The dashed green line marks your median-day composite (p50) — a health reference.
note.optimum: optimum {value} CODL · aim to stay near
heatmap.legend: Daily composite stress · low to high
recs.heading: Patterns detected
recs.disclaimer: Informational only. These signals are behavioral proxies for cognitive load and recovery, not a clinical assessment. For burnout evaluation, the Maslach Burnout Inventory (Maslach & Jackson, 1981) is the validated instrument.
recs.sustained.title: Sustained elevated load: {days} consecutive workdays above your p75
recs.sustained.trigger: composite ≥ your p75 ({p75}) for {days} consecutive days
recs.sustained.advice: Allostatic-load research is clear that cumulative wear comes from the absence of low-load periods, not from peak load alone. Consider a deliberately low-load day in the current week to break the curve.
recs.sustained.citation: McEwen (1998) — Protective and Damaging Effects of Stress Mediators; recovery framing: Sonnentag & Fritz (2007).
recs.offhours.title: Off-hours engagement on {days} days
recs.offhours.trigger: > 0 min of activity outside detected work window on {days} day(s)
recs.offhours.advice: Psychological detachment from work in off-hours predicts next-day vigor and longer-term well-being; off-hours engagement after a heavy week compounds rather than relieves load.
recs.offhours.citation: Sonnentag, Binnewies & Mojza (2010) — Staying well and engaged when demands are high.
recs.fanout.title: Parallel-stream fan-out exceeded WM capacity on {days} days
recs.fanout.trigger: actively-supervised concurrent streams ≥ 4 on {days} workdays (max observed: {max_peak})
recs.fanout.advice: Supervisory-control studies identify fan-out limits where performance degrades non-linearly and subjective stress rises. Sequencing two streams often produces equal throughput with materially lower cognitive cost.
recs.fanout.citation: Cummings & Mitchell (2008) — Predicting Controller Capacity in Supervisory Control of Multiple UAVs; WM grounding: Cowan (2001).
chart.subtitle: Bucketed by local hour · peak {count} simultaneous
tile.details_summary: How this is computed & what it can't tell you
methodology.heading: Methodology & citations
methodology.intro: Generated by ai-code-cognitive-stress {version}. Inputs: agent-coding session transcripts (whichever source adapters are configured via `--source`). {tz_note}
methodology.cache: Cache: {hits} hits, {misses} misses, {write_errors} write errors. {malformed} malformed lines, {no_timestamp} skipped (no timestamp).
methodology.caveats: Caveats inherent to v1: (1) Supervisory-control research was developed for UAV operators, not LLM users — the analogy to LLM oversight is plausible but unvalidated. (2) All axes are taskload (objective demand), not workload (subjective experience); correlation with felt overload is only moderate, and objective and subjective workload are known to dissociate. (3) The Closure Deficit scores resumption load — idle gaps where a parked session was picked back up — using gap duration as a proxy for how cold the loop went. A long autonomous agent turn is excluded (it is not idle), but stepping away mid-turn is not; and the supporting lab evidence measured short (sub-minute) interruptions, so multi-hour and cross-day gaps extrapolate beyond that regime (Parnin & Rugaber 2011 is the closest field bridge). (4) Personal optimum and percentiles require ≥ 14 days of activity; fewer days renders as "calibrating".
methodology.fg_bg: Foreground vs background sessions. A session counts at full CODL weight only while you're actively driving it (within a short grace window of one of your messages). The rest of the time it is alive but "cooking" in the background and counts at a reduced weight (default 0.20) — discounted, because you're not actively tracking it, but never zero: holding a pending intention still costs ~15–20% of ongoing-task capacity (Smith, 2003) and an open goal keeps occupying working memory until it's closed or planned (Masicampo & Baumeister, 2011). So a session you leave running while you step away is penalised lightly, not as if you were juggling it.
methodology.scoring: Composite scoring. The three normalized axes are {scoring_note} Weights and ceilings are configuration parameters (config.json scoring) and can be set from a population calibration over pooled, anonymized exports; the defaults are the literature / null-hypothesis values.
methodology.scoring_default: blended with equal weights (the v1 null hypothesis) and literature normalization ceilings (CODL ÷ 5, interruption ÷ 10/hr).
methodology.scoring_custom: blended with weights CODL/interruption/closure = {w_codl}/{w_interruption}/{w_closure}% and normalization ceilings CODL ÷ {codl_ceiling}, interruption ÷ {interruption_ceiling}/hr — population-calibrated overrides set in config.json, not the v1 equal-weight defaults.
